titleStmt.title: A Transcription of John in Codex Sangermanensis secundus
VL29
The New Testament
The Gospel according to John
john
titleStmt.funder: The Arts and Humanities Research Council

sponsor.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing

publisher.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing
University of Birmingham

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0): available for re-use provided attribution is made to the original creators (the IGNTP and any named participants) and an indication is given of any changes made. See further .

msItem.title: Quattuor Euangelia
Four Gospels in order Mt Mc Lc Jo

surrogates.bibl: Black and white photographs in ITSEE, supplied by Vetus Latina-Institut.

projectDesc.p: This transcription was made by the Verbum Project as part of an online and printed edition of the Old Latin versions of John between 2003 and 2010.

editorialDecl.p: This transcription covers ff. 118v-155r. The manuscript includes an initial list of capitula (Type Pi). This manuscript includes a number of lectionary indications in the body of the text in the first hand, including the formulaic introduction to liturgical readings. The displaced gospel text has sometimes also been included by the first hand. The first hand also includes a few alternative readings introduced by the abbreviation for 'uel' and written above the line. There is inconsistency in the abbreviations (e.g. 'e(st)' indicates a single 'e', but '(est)' is the division sign). There is also much inaccuracy in the copying.

The transcription includes all foliation, lineation and abbreviations. Most punctuation has been included, although punctuation added by a later hand in certain pericopes, probably for liturgical reading, has not been transcribed.The initial transcription file was a plain text file for use with COLLATE. Further information about the procedures followed may be found in the electronic edition at
This second edition of the file was converted to XML by Catherine Smith using the project's Python conversion scripts 1.0.
The XML specifications used are version 1.5 of the IGNTP Guidelines available at

revisionDesc.change: Adjustment of transcription to version 1.5 of the XML by Hugh Houghton.
Reconversion of the original plain text file using Python scripts to version 1.4 of the XML by Catherine Smith and manual checking.
Hyphen entity replaced by lb attribute break="no" by Hugh Houghton
Changes made in order to display properly with VLI.xsl transformation by Hugh Houghton
Change to verse division of 5:18-19 by Hugh Houghton
Transcription converted to the TEI XML schema and corrected for parsing by Hugh Houghton

ab.note: Luke ends on Fol 117v. On Fol. 118r to 118v there is the Monarchian Prologue to John ('hic est iohannis euangelista'). The capitula begin half way down fol. 118v, where the page divides into two columns.
no hang
sic
From now on , the ms is only one column
There is a blank space of ten lines.
The 'd' has been underdotted, and 'fa' written above. The intention was presumably to write 'facta'.
The alternative 'uel recipitis' is written by the first hand above 'non accipitis'.
The alternative 'uel abiit' is added above 'uenit' apparently by the first hand.
The alternative is written above the line, apparently by the first hand.
sic
The alternative 'x' is written above the 'c' of 'dicit'.
The abbreviation could be expanded as 'it(em) ea' or 'it(er)ea' from 'interea' with a missing 'n'.
There is an illegible word in the gutter: perhaps an abbreviation for 'huius'?.
The word 'Et' appears to have been erased.
There is an 'o' above 'uiii', presumably to indicate 'octo'.
sic
sic
sic
The alternative is added above the line by the first hand.
sic
The first line of this page appears to be in a different hand.
The abbreviation for 'uel' is above 'his'; however, the 'q' has the abbreviations for both 'qui' and 'quae', which appears to be the alternative reading.
A later hand has added + signs either side of ih¬s, which appears to be a deletion.
There is an 'a' above xxx, presumably to indicate 'triginta'.
sic
The alternative is written above the line.
sic
sic
sic
sic
written as p(er)p(er)
sic
The double p(er) abbreviation by the copyist (as seen in 6:59) has been expanded by the addition of 'rot' over the line, to indicate 'propter'.
The additional letters have been added above the line.
sic
sic
The alternative 'uel scit' is added above the line.
sic
The 'e' of 'sed' has an upward curve, although this is not a known abbreviation.
The copyist has misread 'quisque' as 'quinque'.
sic
The 'e' of 'medio' has an upward curve, although this is not a known abbreviation.
The 'e' of 'ego' has an upward curve.
The abbreviation is for 'is', as in 'discipul(is)' or 'uob(is)'.
sic
Later annotation in right margin 'i(d est) o xc¬'.
sic
The alternative is written above the line.
There are circular marks above the 'n' and 'e' of 'nescio' which appear to signify a deletion.
sic
The alternative is written above the line.
The 'd' of 'quando' has a horizontal line as for an abbreviation.
The abbreviation, a line through 'l', indicates 'is' elsewhere: as 'oculos' is found in full on this page, '-is' has been retained here, but the abbreviation may have been intended to signify '-los'.
There may be some erased characters at the end of this line.
There may be some erased characters at the end of this line.
There is an ink mark above the line, but it does not appear to obscure a missing 'scio'.

There is a + sign either side of 'discipuli', and the final two letters of 'moysi' have been erased. It is not clear what the corrector intended: a reordering in keeping with the Stuttgart Vulgate has been taken as the most likely intention.
The correction is poorly written.
'peccatores' has been reinterpreted as 'peccator es'.
sic
The form, but not apparently the reading, of 'proprias' has been changed.
sic
sic
sic
'or' above 'iiii' to indicate 'quattuor'.
sic
sic
sic
The alternative is added above the line.
sic
The alternative has been added above the line, but 'turbam' has been deleted, apparently by a different hand.
The alternative is added above the line, but 'igitur' was subsequently deleted.
sic
sic
The alternative is added above the line.
sic
sic
sic
sic
sic
The alternative to 'perdiderit' is written above 'qui'.
The alternative to 'filium' is written above 'tuum Uenit'.
sic
sic
sic
The abbreviation is for (is), as in the following word.
sic
The correction is written in the form of an alternative 'uel io' above the line, with the letters 'abo' deleted.
The alternative is written over 'habet', possibly not by the first hand.
Damage to the photograph means that these characters are illegible on the images available to us.
sic
Damage to the photograph means that 'iunt' is not visible in the image available to us.
The digit '·IIII·' is written in the bottom margin; possibly a quire signature.
No running title is visible.
A line of abbreviation is over the 'p' of 'resp', but there is then a gap of two characters.
sic
sic
The copyist appears to have written 'martris' using a capital for the second 'r'. Alternatively, the reading may be 'martius'.
sic
There are dots above and below the first bow of the 'x'. presumably to turn it into a 'c'.
Some letters may have been erased at the end of this line.
There is no running title.
sic
sic
There is no running title.
The alternative is written above the line.
On the last two lines of this page is written, in red capitals, "incipiunt capitulare euangeliorum de circulo anni".
